content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Obsessed with time travel movies. What else would you recommend?

**Score:** 116
**Body:** Here are the ones I’ve watched 

Palm Springs 
Beyond the infinite two minutes 
Timecrimes 
12 Monkeys
Source Code 
Butterfly effect 
Coherence
Predestination 
Looper 

What else would you recommend? TIA 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Time After Time (1979)
2. Since you’ve already covered most of the popular ones, try:

**Primer** – probably the most realistic and confusing take on time travel  
**Triangle** – more of a psychological loop, really underrated  
**About Time** – lighter, emotional, but still uses time travel really well  
**Donnie Darko** – weird, but it sticks with you  
**Edge of Tomorrow** (if you’re okay with a loop style again)

Also if you’re open to shows, **Dark** is basically peak time travel storytelling.
3. * DARK (It's a TV Show and the best one for time travel)
* About Time
* Predestination
* Caddo Lake (Watch this after you've watched Dark)
4. The Time Machine (1960), absolutely worth a watch even though you have to be a little forgiving due to its age. Pretty sure it’s free on yt 
5. Primer (2004) is a solid pick! 
  - OP, this is the best answer and it's gonna twist your brain all the way into a knot.
6. Bill and Ted's Excellent Adventure!
7. The Final Countdown (1980)
8. For something different to the list, how about Peggy Sue got Married 
9. X-Men: Days of Future Past

Star Trek IV: The Voyage Home

The Terminator 

## 2. I hope I didn't finish every single dumb comedy movie I could watch

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I'm looking for comedy movies with no filters, I like really dumb comedy movies like most of the ones listed below that I really enjoyed:

Edit: No 90's or soft comedy movies like Eurovision or The 40-Year-Old Virgin etc..., I'm looking for something more stupid and crazy

\- Pineapple Express

\- Tropic Thunder

\- Superbad

\- Pineapple Express

\- Sausage Party

\- Your Highness

\- The Interview

\- This Is 40

\- Neighbors (+ Neighbors 2)

\- The Night Before

\- Ted (+ Ted 2 and Ted series)

\- Hot Rod

\- All the hangover movies

\- Hot Tub Time Machine

\- This is the end

\- Year One

\- Forgetting Sarah Marshall

\- Get Him to the Greek

\- The Dirt: Mötley Crüe

\- Paul

\- Land of the Lost

\- Vacation

\- We're the Millers

\- The Wrong Missy

\- The Dictator

\- Borat

\- A Milion Ways to Die in the West

\- Do the Over

\- Game Over Man

\- Father of the Year

\- War Dogs

\- Wolf of Wallstreet

\- The Ridiculous 6

\- Dirty Grandpa

\- Project X

\- Role Models

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. MacGruber is a huge omission
2. Naked Gun series, Air Plane, Top Secret!
3. Spaceballs and Blazing Saddles
4. Tucker and Dale vs evil ? 
5. Hamlet 2?
6. Grandma's Boy
7. The Other Guys. 
8. Kung Pow: Enter the Fist \
Shaolin Soccer \
Kung Fu Hustle \
Tromaville series \
Sharknado series \
Thankskilling (with killer turkey) \
Rubber (about the tire) \
Jackass series \
Velocipastor \
Bruno \
Undercover Brother
9. The other guys
10. I think *Role Models* would fit in here

## 3. Anything remotely similar to Blue Ruin?

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Just watched Blue Ruin for the first time this past week, maybe more than once 😉 , any recommendations for similar in-depth, very personal, slow roller (to a point at least) style films? 

My movie style is slowly changing as I get older I guess. So this, Aftersun, All of Us Strangers, and Civil War have all really peaked a new interest into indy-styles for me recently.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t think it has the same vibe but Green Room (by the same director) rocks
2. I don't remember Blue Ruin all that well, but I think Dead Man's Shoes has some similar vibes
3. Blue Ruin is a 10/10 for me. Check out more Saulnier’s:
- green room
- rebel ridge (this one especially)

- I don’t feel at home in this world anymore (dir by Macon Blair, star of Blue Ruin) has a lot to offer in feel and sensibility.

- Mud (by Jeff Nichols)
- Deliverance
- Marathon Man
- Stray Dogs


4. Just so you know, the word is "piqued" and not peaked.


Any Macon Blair or Saulnier pics will scratch that itch. Lynne Ramsey movies as well.
5. Yeah!You would probably love..

 "Green Room"

"What You Wish"

"Sweet Home Virginia"

"Red White & Blue" (This will leave you feeling kind of dirty after)

"What Keeps You Alive"



All are cant-miss!
6. Hell or high water is a slow-to-medium burn kind of violent movie, where it's over the top but real and personnal at the same time.

No Country For Old Men is a good one too.
7. Bit of a dartboard throw but:

* Cold in July
* True Romance
* Dinner in America

Edit:

* Love Lies Bleeding
  - I love True Romance but that's quite the opposite kind of movie
8. The same year’s It Follows.
9. Bad Turn Worse

Shotgun Stories

Ain’t Them Bodies Saints

Out of the Furnace 

## 4. Strong female lead in romance

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I’m actually sick of female leads often portrayed as jolly, chatty, & with super high energy. One movie I can easily think of is People we meet on vacation. i know it’s from a book. Don’t get me wrong, overall the movie’s okay (and i like bader) but really? the girl dancing and singing in public, in an airport?! cringe. 

I think this stems from my personality? I don’t really resonate with the common female leads that talks more than the male lead.

Some movies I approve of are:  
\- 10 things i hate about you  
\- crazy rich asians  
\- sleeping with other people  
\- comrades almost a love story  
\- palm springs  
\- together  
\- the proposal

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Out of Africa 

The Guernsey Literary and Potato Peel Society

Witness 

Eat Drink Man Woman

Lost in Translation

Pride & Prejudice (2005)

Loving (2016)
 
Miss Potter

The Age of Innocence

Hidden Figures

Enchanted April

A Good Year

Sliding Doors

The Bridges of Madison County

Casablanca
2. While You Were Sleeping 

Take Me Home

The Giant Mechanical Man

My Big Fat Greek Wedding 
  - While You Were Sleeping is one of my favorite movies of all time. 
3. Julie Delpy in the Linklater “Before” movies.
  - I was going to suggest this. Before Sunrise, Before Sunset, Before Midnight.  Fantastic trilogy
4. Brooklyn
5. Are you willing to go “classic”? Barbara Stanwyck kills it in “The Lady Eve”.
6. I hate that too. Have you seen Lady Bird? More coming of age than romance, and she definitely talks a lot, but she’s pessimistic. 
7. Muriel's Wedding
8. Ivy Walker in The Village is my favourite example of this since no one even considers that movie a romance in the first place 

## 5. Psychological Thriller suggestions

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Wassup y'all. I'm a sucker for mind bending movies, movies that make me question what I've been watching. Please drop some of your favourites...

Not Oldboy, Shutter Island, Mulholland Drive, Triangle, Gone Girl, The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo, Fractured or Irreversible. I've seen those

Give me your best shit! Something you KNOW for a fact will be worth me sitting my ass down, popping some popcorn and investing my limited time to consume. Preferably more on the traumatic side, like I wanna take 15 minutes to really take in the whole thing when I'm done. Thank you. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Angel Heart (1987). Don't google this one, just watch it. 
2. Frailty 
3. A Simple Plan, Primal Fear, The Vanishing (1988)
4. Predestination obviously. Will fk your brain. 

Loved the film Anna. Great acting and twists. Psychological thriller? Oh yeah absolutely. 

Memento of course. 

Identity with Cusack.

Side Effects...really enjoyed that one.
5. Anatomy of a Fall 

A perfect getaway 

Mr Brooks 

Spinning Man 

The secret in their eyes 

Fracture ( Ryan Gosling) 

Black butterfly 

The gift ( Rebecca Hall)

The night house 
 
6. The Odessa File

Last of the Dogmen 
7. The wicker man

Picnic at hanging rock

8. Great choices! You may have seen these too, but they come to my mind:

The Witch

Saint Maud

Mandy

Lost Highway 

Twin Peaks: Fire Walk With Me 

Donnie Darko

Invasion of the Body Snatchers, 1978

(I realized some may not consider these to be psychological thrillers, but they felt mind-bending and well-written to me.)
9. mother! (2018)

You've never seen anything like it.
10. Midsommar  2019

Black Swan

Caveat

The Menu

The Cell

Audition

One Hour Photo

Enemy  2013

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. [Question] In this scene of Tenet, The Protagonist wears an oxygen mask because he is inverted and can't breathe the oxygen of the world running opposite to him. So why dosen't he have to wear some sort of suit to protect the atoms of his body as well?

**Score:** 944
**Body:** aren't the atoms that make up his body inverted as well, and would be affected by the world running opposite to them?

wouldn't two atoms running opposite/inverted to each other cancel out or explode when coming in contact with each other?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pretty sure it's just an excuse to have a visual cue for which characters are inverted.  If they went any further, the actors would be unrecognizable.
  - Spot on its a logic sacrificial lamb nolan gives the audience one specific rule to track masks inverted so he doesn’t have to explain the quantum physics of his clothes or skin
    - yah and he's said so many times the movie isn't about making sense, it's about enjoying it in the moment. long ago my brother said to me mid critique 'not every movie has to be a documentary.' and he's absolutely right.

aside from realism though, yes it's just a visual/audience queue to the inverted world. don't think about it so much everyone, just enjoy the journey.
2. Might as well ask how they carry around days and days worth of oxygen with them.  The little tiny tanks they show couldn’t last more than 5m.
  - I would be more worried about having to poop.
    - Imagine the poop just running up the pipes, shooting out of the toilet bowl straight into your opening rectum.
      - https://preview.redd.it/56abrcs122zg1.jpeg?width=868&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=665def3d0b7f5c6d535b130cb12489a36fb09354
3. Fuck you thats why
  - I love the idea of this subreddit having this exact energy for everything. 
4. we kinda went over a lot of this when the movie came out. it isn’t wrong to like this movie and it isn’t wrong to dislike it. 

but, however you feel about this movie, any questions you have can usually be answered by the fact that it frankly just doesn’t make any sense.

## 2. What's your perfect movie? For me it's the thing.

**Score:** 741
**Body:** Every film has flaws. But what's the perfect movie for you? For me it's the thing. I can watch this flick all the time and find new stuff. Everything is perfect here. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terminator 2 is pretty dang solid
2. Fellowship of the Ring

I love the lotr trilogy, but Fellowship is magnificent. 
3. Mad Max Fury Road 
4. ![gif](giphy|z8lV4Mx6NyhtS)
5. Tremors 
6. Galaxy Quest

![gif](giphy|7fCfc8ts4v1HKtgRcM|downsized)
7. HEAT
8. Jurassic Park  
Literally me every time the T-Rex breaks out

![gif](giphy|37Fsl1eFxbhtu)
9. Jaws, with The Thing a close second
10. ![gif](giphy|13UZxmMyyFr584)

## 3. [Question] Did Harvey's character arc feel rushed to you? It felt like he was too good of a person to become a villain just like that.

**Score:** 537
**Body:** he goes from being a pure soul clean conscience lawyer who will do anything for Gotham to a lunatic killing people at a coin toss, in the span of what, two days?

i mean i can understand that he felt vulnerable and cornered after Rachel was murdered but still, killing the child of an innocent man who could've in no way prevented any of that?

it felt too fast given how strong willed he was prior to Joker.

maybe they should have introduced him in the previous movie as Bruce's friend, then the Joker incident happens in the next one and we see him struggling to cope through it and then in third one he comes as a side villain.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think it actually made sense because of how perfect he was in the first half. He sees the world in black and white (or, dare I say, heads or tails). 

Before the accident he is just as relentless and obsessed with justice, he’s just fighting the same people Batman is so we want to root for him.

Then his fiancé dies and he is horribly disfigured for life, largely due to the incompetence of the Gotham police. So he takes that same obsession with justice and points at the new people he believes deserve punishment.

He has to be perfect first. It’s what makes the Two-Face character work. 
  - Great comment here. Once he found himself becoming a villain, he really owned it. Guy couldn’t stop himself from being the extreme version of his own superego. Kinda like Bruce Wayne. Makes you wonder what would happen if their situations were reversed.
  - that's an interesting perspective!  
didn't think of that.  
thanks for adding!
2. Yeah a bit. But they had to do it within a small amount of time in a film with a lot going on. He was almost a background villain due to the story with the Joker. 
3. He shouldn't have been so flawless in the first half of the movie maybe
  - He wasn't flawless.  They stuck in lines suggesting he had a darker side from his history in internal affairs, and was a bit machiavellian (ends justify the means) and Batman has to stop him in the first half when he kidnaps and threatens to kill an assassin in a secret interrogation (albeit with a two sided heads coin so it is implied he wasn't intending to murder him).
    - Mhmm, they do mention that he had the nickname of "Harvey Two-Face" in law school or something like that. 
      - When he worked for internal affairs
4. Way too rushed. Deserved another film really 
5. Yes 

## 4. Cillian Murphy in 28 Days Later is amazing. Him going from a calm, mild mannered survivor to a rage filled badass never fails to give me chills

**Score:** 491
**Body:** 28 days later remains one of the best zombie flicks,  but it’s Cillians performance that stands out.

Him going berserk in the end to save his team lives rent free in my head.

The “in the house in a heartbeat” soundtrack and the dark aesthetic of the scene is just spot on. 

Some of the best character development in a horror movie.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of the best character developments I’ve ever seen in a movie, hands down. 
  - “That was longer than a heartbeat.”

I don’t know why, but that line gets me emotionally. Earlier in the film Selena tells Jim she will kill him in a heartbeat if he gets infected. 

That line brings Selena’s arc in full circle and shows that even in a broken, virus ridden world love and connection can still exist.
  - This and District 9 really never got topped for me in terms of sci-fi thriller character arcs.
2. He’s a great actor, and as ageless as Paul Rudd in his way
3. Yeah, his arc is what elevates this film beyond being just a good zombie horror flick.
  - Agreed.

Him taking a stand and returning to save Selena and Hannah always gets me hyped no matter how many times I rewatch this!
4. ![gif](giphy|4jtEWnmdELhOU)
5. I remember watching this and The Wind That Shakes the Barley then I was totally sold on this guy. 
6. He shows the full monty as well...
  - Dude hangs dong

## 5. Michael Shannon is terrific as a villain. His intense presence and quiet menace make him genuinely chilling and unforgettable on screen. What’s your favorite character played by him?

**Score:** 321

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Boardwalk Empire
2. Like every other day I get the opportunity to recommend 

Take shelter (2011)

Like he is the victim of himself as a villain somehow 
3. https://i.redd.it/ctbrtkxvm4zg1.gif

I don’t know if he was actually behind the mask in these flashbacks, but the White Death in Bullet Train was terrifying
  - That movie was so much fun
4. Also a great villain in Premium Rush.
5. Not a villain but Bug was another level
6. Iceman & 99 homes
7. President James Garfield, Death by Lighting. He was terrific. 

Though it will always Boardwalk Empire, Nelson Van Alden. 
8. Take Shelter

Midnight Special 

Both of my favorites with him 
9. His monologue as a Sorority Head is the best
  https://youtu.be/dngOH9G4UPw?si=Hu2jAj0BmHFc6LN7

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Is Quentin Tarantino correct about biopic films? “They are just big excuses for actors to win Oscars. It's a corrupted cinema”

**Score:** 514
**Body:** As someone who has worked in the film industry, I joke that once you start working on films “based on a true story,” you have officially achieved the “Hollywood Dream.” You are no longer working on shitty horror films, but rather shitty biopics instead 💪

From a production standpoint, I think Tarantino is correct, but I view it more as “creating easy jobs for ourselves.” But definitely a byproduct is that it results in “lesser” or corrupted cinema. At the same time though, biographies are actually harder to produce because you have to hire lawyers, historians, and PR teams just to ensure no one is angry enough to sue or create controversy. 

I believe this is the main reason biopics are such a homogeneous and “safe” genre, because at the end of the day, the people in suits are the ones deciding everything. Eventually, all these films end up becoming and feeling the same.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fame based biopics I (generally) agree. There isn't anything inherently interesting or noteworthy about famous actors or singers, they're basically people who won a lottery. Because there's no genuine "there" there they've become formulaic bores, the struggle, the success, the excess of success, the comeback, blah blah blah, and ultimately they're just vehicles for actors to do impressions that will impress award voters and studios bankroll because famous people have enough name recognition to bring in an audience. 

Biopics about genuine moments or figures of history, Oppenheimer, Lincoln, even Social Network, are different though, cause they're telling a story based in the idea that the significance of the story itself is compelling. The still might be awards baity, but there's an intention to them that's truer to story telling than the cash grab imitation theater of stuff like Michael or Ray. 
  - I find that a convincing performance of a real person is a bit like a photorealistic painting: It’s technically impressive but not necessarily artistically interesting.
2. I think he’s right, but you have to qualify the kind of biopic he’s talking about to a narrow category of “biopic.” 

Is “The Imitation Game” a biopic or a true story about enigma with added focus to Alan Turing? 

Because I think Imitation is a good to very good movie in a way Bohemian Rhapsody, Michael, or the Eddie Redmayne Steven Hawking film are not. 

Lincoln is essentially a biopic as it is all about one true person… but it’s also not one, because it’s about the time as much as it is about the man and the narrative holds to that. 

I think it could be boiled down to “stories where the entirety of a persons life is summarized for a 2 hour film are tiresome and predictable and yet the academy tends to love them.”
  - Imitation Game is arguably a biopic insofar as many of them only focus on one specific crucial event or period of the life of the person represented. It's also an awful one as far as I'm concerned. Virtually all of it is made up or bent from the historical truth so much that it would have been better to make it a fantasy story rather than leave anyone with the impression that they learned anything at all about the real Turing.
    - Yeah, Imitation Game is the worst kind of biopic - it actively distorts history, makes villains from good people and erases work of many good people. 

It's worse than something thats vapid and boring - it's actively harmful.
    -  People who were led to Turing by the movie and researched his actual accomplishments found themselves pretty disappointed I think
  - They are essentially “slice of life” biopics. Any format can be good, as long as the filmmakers stick to it over a “jack of all trades” style of biopic where it becomes a mess. 

I do wish Lincoln wasn’t called Lincoln though, Selma (2014) wasn’t called “King” 

Edit: Here’s some fringe but interesting film history. In the Soviet Union, there was a series of propaganda films (I joke, “the Leninwalker saga”) about certain moments of Vladimir Lenin’s life. (Stalin got the the same treatment)

“Lenin in October” “Lenin in 1918” “Stories about Lenin” “Lenin in Poland” “Lenin in Paris” 

The first two were made by the same filmmakers in the 30s, while the next two also had one set of filmmakers making them in the 50s/60s. The last one was made by the same director as before but in the 1980s. 

Obviously made for propaganda, but from an artistic standpoint, I think they are pretty interesting. Imagine a filmmaker in the US making disjointed history films, but all about MLK. 

“King in Alabama” “King in Prison” “King in Washington DC” “King in Church” “King in 1968”

One film is about King’s time in prison, another is about his Christian faith, and another is about the year of his death. There’s nothing “plot” connecting them but the audience is just left to interpret why a filmmaker would make biopics this way.
  - There are biopics (shallow retelling of a Wikipedia page) and there are biopics (the story of a person's life and what it says about the world they lived in). 

There are biopics like Raging Bull and Malcolm X that are at the forefront of cinema and then there's stuff like Michael, which is basically advertisment.
3. While most biopics are quite bad, there are also film like Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters, Capote and The End of the Tour (incidentally all three are about writers), which do something interesting with the genre. 

As always, QT’s tendency for blanket statements makes him seem like a jackass, when all he had to do was say "most biopics". 
4. When considering what films are made for ”bad” reasons, imo the easiest way to evaluate the situation is to look at the intention of the key creative people on the project, especially the director.

Is the director here because it is an artistic passion project? If no, the project is very likely to be made for mostly questionable reasons.

Some actors absolutely want to play a specific person though, and this is the big reason. 

At the end of the day, I definitely understand why especially actors would want to do biopics. The roles are usually very meaty, and the film is focused on the character. it is simply a good role by many metrics. Great roles are hard to come by, especially if you are not one of the 20 most sought after actors.

## 2. "Barry Lyndon" is IMO one perfect little scene after the next.

**Score:** 95
**Body:** I think this might be Kubrick's most perfect colour film. Of all his long flicks, it has the best pacing IMO, and every scene feels like an interesting and unique little vignette.

It also strikes me that the film is like an epic version of Kubrick's "The Killing". "The Killing", we recall, was a noir about how the present is always deterministically (indeterministically?) influenced by the past, how even the best plans can't fully overcome this, and how hard free will is an illusion. Indeed, in both films, horses, lucky horse shoes, and games of chance/luck are used to signify the moments when human illusions of control begin to derail.

You get this same sense in "Barry Lyndon". Barry tries to be the master of his fate - and thinks he is - but almost every scene stresses how events are actually out of his hands. He thinks he's won his first duel, for example, but the whole thing was orchestrated to go a certain way. Later, when marching into a hail of bullets, it is just sheer blind luck that determines who gets shot or not. 

Throughout the film, Kubrick stresses how arbitrary are the events which define a life. And how forces are always conspiring behind the scenes to influence a person's path. The film then ends with Barry trying to push back against this - luck rolls in his favor and he tries to make what he thinks is a free personal choice, shooting his pistol into the ground - and he's punished for this.

Throughout the film, you see references to gambling, games of chance, games of luck, and so on, and Kubrick's camera work emphasizes this as well. The camera is always zooming out, pulling away until characters are dwarfed by an environment that they're controlled by, but which their egos, vanities and arrogance prevent them from acknowledging. 

(we see here why Kubrick was drawn to his "Napoleon" project: Napoleon naively thought he could play God and micromanage the universe)

There's also some political subtext sprinkled about. "Barry Lyndon's" a film about social climbers, people scheming for money, land, titles and power, and like "Shining" and "Eyes Wide Shut" is about an outsider who mingles with the upper classes. Ending on the date of the French Revolution, it also seems to suggest that this is a dead-end. Everyone in the film is chasing "satisfaction", but their desires are the causes of their suffering. They're also all looking for something which transcends their own mortality, but nothing does. Indeed, the film continually stresses that all these people are already dead, and a moving coffin is the only thing in the film able to outpace its slow zoom outs. Death, in a sense, is the only thing that is constant and immortal.

Kubrick reportedly was influenced by Schopenhauer's "The Vanity of Existence" when writing "Barry Lyndon". And you see these influences everywhere. It's a film obsessed with characters who are always "becoming" and never "being", and who are so vain (always dolling themselves up etc) that they miss how small they are on their own canvas.

Quoting some passages from a Jim Emerson essay (titled "The Cosmic Wager") on Roger Ebert's website, which I thought were relevant:

>"Barry is a prisoner of mise-en-scene, trapped in a work of art, just as all we creatures who suffer from self-awareness are imprisoned by our Darwinian destinies. Somewhere, over eons of evolution or at the subatomic level of quantum physics, everything breaks down and becomes random — or predetermined or both. Einstein famously avowed that “God does not play dice.” Human beings have no choice."

>"Which brings us to art, and man’s attempts to play God. Watching the wryly comic adventures of Barry Lyndon from a huge (one might even say cosmic) ironic distance, you begin to sense that Kubrick is both ennobling and immortalizing this fictional rascal through the very act of focusing his attention on him on film. [...] After all, on the grand scale of things, Barry’s plight is ours."

>"...it is the landscape, rather than the people who inhabit it, that dominates the frame. Though man does not yet hold complete dominion over nature... [...] Everything in “Barry Lyndon” is similarly “arranged” by unseen hands to fit some preordained design. Sometimes this design is arranged by the characters (Nora’s marriage to, and Barry’s duel with, Captain Quin; the Chevalier de Balibari’s escape to England; Barry’s and Chevalier’s “luck” at cards), by predestination or fate (the deaths of Grogan, Bryan, and Sir Charles Lyndon; the singular “accident” which enables Barry to escape the army)...."

> ...the elements in each shot are locked together in a precise pattern within the frame, increasing the sense of design and destiny. [...] a world in which every man/object has its place and nothing can be out of place. It is a world clamped tightly inside a rectangle."

>"Kubrick’s frame keeps Barry boxed in, more so even that the rigid social conventions of his time and place. The three times Kubrick does allow his camera to become directly involved in the action, it is within a carefully defined, four-cornered space: the boxing match (“We will form a square for that purpose”); Lady Lyndon’s suicide attempt (in her beautifully-furnished bedroom); and Barry’s physical attack on Lord Bullingdon (in an echoing, hardwood-floored concert chamber)."

>"In each case, the box or the square signifies a social arena in which some conflict is played out. 

>"Barry’s attempts to master his fate and behave — or at least appear to behave — like a gentleman are quite touching in the light of his inevitable failure. He fights duels, pitting his own skill against the whims of fate. He assists the Chevalier at cards — and what better, more pathetic metaphor for Man’s attempts to master chance/fate than those of a gambler and con artist cheating in an effort to master “the odds” [...] however, the narrator informs us that Barry will lose everything: “Fate had determined that he should leave none of his race behind him, and that he should die alone and penniless ….” [...] Character, fate, birth-these elements work as one, like the mechanisms of a watch, keeping Barry’s life ticking inexorably toward its conclusion."

>"But Barry does achieve one great moment of existential triumph, which also happens to coincide with the moment of his ultimate destruction. They are one and the same. In the duel with Lord Bullingdon, Barry, for once in his life, behaves honorably and like a gentleman — indeed he does not actually become a gentleman until the moment when he fires his pistol into the ground instead of at Lord Bullingdon. For an instant, Barry holds his fate in his own hands: He can do away with Bullingdon forever and still be playing strictly by the rules of proper conduct, or he can leave his fate up to Bullingdon. Barry, who was so moved by the aesthetic splendor of the Chevalier’s appearance, here displays a kind of aesthetic style and grace that, even without an officially recognized title, is the mark of a gentleman. It is Bullingdon who is vulgar, who behaves dishonorably, when he hasn’t the good grace to consider the matter “honorably settled.” Gloriously, paradoxically, Barry attains his measure of nobility — and humanity — at the very moment of losing everything else. (This incident, by the way, is Kubrick’s — not Thackeray’s.)"

>"It is his purest act of free will as a human being … and it seals his fate, consigns him to oblivion — a terrible lot that is bitterly ironic and profoundly touching."

>"We last see Barry as he climbs into another box — a coach — and Kubrick stops him cold in a freeze-frame. After this moment, the narrator tells us, Barry disappears into oblivion: “His life on the Continent we have not the means of following accurately; but he appears to have resumed his former profession of a gambler, but without his former success.” No less than “The Shining”‘s Jack Torrance (who also ends up frozen-in ice and a freeze-frame, and then in a still photograph from 1921) the tragedy of “Barry Lyndon” is that he has become a human figure forever frozen in Stanley Kubrick’s time-frame."


Anyway, I thought this film was a masterpiece  (I personally think it's Kubrick's best color film, with "Eyes Wide Shut" next). The only other film I've seen like this is Kurosawa's powerful "Ran".

## 3. Is Possession in answer to Repulsion? Placing Polanski's Repulsion (1965) and Zulawski's Possession (1981) in relationship to each other

**Score:** 21
**Body:** a redditor lightly compared these two films by Polish directors and I was surprised by how much sense it makes to put them in relationship to each other. The more I thought about it Possession reads perhaps as an answer to Repulsion, not just as a film, but also philosophically, psychologically and spiritually. Repulsion is the murderous and paranoid retreat from oppressive urban male gaze/desire on a beautiful woman - one might say - a **contraction** into the solitary urban apartment, and Possession reads as the opposite, the murderous, psychotic **expansion** in the face of domestic banality and the pressures of urban, low middle class wife-motherhood, fleeing the apartment into an affair and then ecstatic self-creation (that to me reads as a female **artistic** response - notably Anna fashions her "escape" out of her own body, the demon-lover made from her miscarriage, something Polanski's trapped Carol cannot do). Both are horror films, both entail the oppressive pressures on a woman and the "break" from them. Zulawski has struck me as subtly in tension or even outright animosity with the artistically embraced fellow pole Polanski. Is his Possession, as a kind of sequel, an attempt to overcome Polanski's prodigy film Repulsion with his own French cinema beauty? At the very least the question does being out some possible, deeper philosophical themes of Possession, and what it means to break free, what lines of flight are possible.

But in any case, it kind of ruined Repulsion  me personally. Possession, in the other hand, has more layers to uncover every time I watch it.

I think Possession has also aged much better than what most thought upon its release. It feels like Zulawski's overall vision of film is better understood, and has become more accessible upon reflection.

## 4. A.I. Artificial Intelligence (2001)

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Inspired by a recent thread, I thought I'd start a discussion of a pretty fascinating, polarizing film.

When it came out, it underperformed at the box office and got mixed reviews, with the discourse dominated by the question of how much of it is Kubrick's vs. Spielberg's. 

(For what it's worth, based on everything I read I think it's on the whole pretty true to Kubrick's original vision. He was getting schmaltzier as an old man, telling his circle about his love of Pinocchio and desire to make a film for children. And he was more involved in what became the final film than you might think; he directed Robin Williams's voice acting performance (the last time he ever directed an actor) and approved the concept art that informed the film's design.)

It's a film that underwhelmed and somewhat baffled me when I first saw it, but also a film that I've really come to appreciate on subsequent viewings. To me, the mix of tones (somewhat child-friendly Pinocchio retelling in a dystopian world of sexbots and anti-robot prejudice) gives it a really unique personality that stands out from every other science fiction movie. A pretty unique mix of ingredients. 

From 2001 on, Kubrick was really interested in films divided into discrete sections, and AI is no exception. The juxtaposition from the end of the first section (David and Teddy lost in the woods) straight to Gigolo Joe selling a client on his robotic lovemaking abilities feels vintage Kubrick comedy. 

Stanley Kubrick thought that the protagonist David would be some kind of special effect, but I think that Haley Joel Osment gives one of the great child acting performances in the role. He really goes from creepy and uncanny valley at the beginning to a character that feels extremely *human*. 

The ending was the part that really put me off when I first watched it. I honestly thought it would end with>! David in the submarine staring at the Blue Fairy statue in sunken New York for eternity!<. But I think the ending is keeping with the sense of wonder of 2001, that it does something interesting by putting this story in a gigantic, cosmic context.

 This film has been reappraised, not just by me but by quite a few people. It made last years' NYT top 100 films of this century. Part of it has to be the world we live in, which is increasingly AI-dominated. It's a film, on its most high-concept level, about AIs becoming more and more like humans.

5. One of my favorites movies; I used to watch it all the time on DVD as a kid and last year I finally saw it in a theater for the first time. 

I used to hate the ending as well because I didn't like the "happy" ending, but as I've gotten older I've not only come around on it but after the time I saw it in the theater I now find it to be the true "not happy" ending that I always said I wanted. More melancholy and contemplative, which feels correct.   
  
Because in the water David gets to hold on to the illusion of magic, to his childish dream of being a real boy for Monica, forever. He gets to live in a fantasy, albeit while frozen underwater. When they dig him out of the ice, however, he's directly confronted with an entirely new reality and he not only has to say goodbye he's also now without purpose since he was only ever programmed to love her + her family. It's also unclear to me if this new Monica only loves him because now they're both artificial beings; would the first Monica have ever given him this moment of closure and said that she loved him? And regardless of any of that, she's also the last human ever, or at the very least the last memory of one. It opens the story back up in a lot of interesting ways that I hadn't considered when I was younger; in ways that get me really thinking about life and existence and what it means to love.
